research.interface: Snowflake SQL API v2, POST/GET /api/v2/statements (GA; jsonv2 result format)

official_sources.title: Snowflake SQL API reference
official_sources.used_for: POST /api/v2/statements and GET /api/v2/statements/{statementHandle}; requestId/async/retry query parameters; request body fields (statement, timeout, database, schema, warehouse, role, bindings); ResultSet/QueryStatus/QueryFailureStatus schemas; status codes 200/202/408/422/429; codes 090001 and 333334

official_sources.title: Submitting a request to execute SQL statements
official_sources.used_for: required Authorization/Content-Type/Accept/User-Agent headers, requestId UUID plus retry=true resubmission semantics, bindings structure {"1": {"type": ..., "value": ...}} with string-encoded values, binding types FIXED/REAL/TEXT/BOOLEAN

official_sources.title: Handling responses
official_sources.used_for: 202 QueryStatus with statementHandle/statementStatusUrl, polling GET /api/v2/statements/{handle} until 200/422, resultSetMetaData (numRows, format, rowType), data rows encoded as arrays of strings with JSON null for SQL NULL

official_sources.title: Authenticating to the server
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Bearer plus X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type header with KEYPAIR_JWT/OAUTH/PROGRAMMATIC_ACCESS_TOKEN values

official_sources.title: Getting details about an error
official_sources.used_for: 408 returned with a QueryStatus body when the statement exceeds the request timeout; 422 returned with a QueryFailureStatus body (code, sqlState, message, statementHandle) when execution fails

research.language_rationale: Snowflake positions the SQL API exactly for driverless custom integrations; the documented surface is plain REST/JSON, so Python's stdlib urllib/http stack is the idiomatic zero-dependency client for a job runner that cannot ship the native connector.

verified_facts: Statements are submitted with POST /api/v2/statements under https://{account_identifier}.snowflakecomputing.com/api; the body carries statement, timeout (seconds), database, schema, warehouse, role, bindings and parameters, all optional except statement in practice, with case-sensitive context names.

Every request needs Authorization: Bearer {token}, Accept: application/json, a real User-Agent, and Content-Type: application/json on POST; X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type declares the token kind (KEYPAIR_JWT, OAUTH or PROGRAMMATIC_ACCESS_TOKEN) and Snowflake infers it when omitted.

requestId is a UUID query parameter; resubmitting the same request after an ambiguous failure with the SAME requestId plus retry=true tells the server it is a resubmission so the statement is not executed twice.

Bind variables are sent as bindings: an object keyed by 1-based position, each entry {type, value} where value is always a string (e.g. "123" for 123, "true" for TRUE); documented types include FIXED, REAL, TEXT, BOOLEAN, DATE, TIME and the TIMESTAMP_* family.

A 200 response is a ResultSet: code 090001, sqlState 00000, message, statementHandle, statementStatusUrl, resultSetMetaData {numRows, format jsonv2, rowType[{name,type,length,precision,scale,nullable}], partitionInfo}, and data as an array of rows whose values are strings or JSON null for SQL NULL.

A 202 response is a QueryStatus (code 333334, asynchronous execution in progress) carrying statementHandle and statementStatusUrl; the client polls GET /api/v2/statements/{handle} and each poll returns 202 again, 200 with the ResultSet, or 422 on failure.
If execution exceeds the timeout in the request (or STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS), the server answers 408 with a QueryStatus body, so the handle is still recoverable from a timed-out submission.
Execution errors answer 422 with a QueryFailureStatus: code, sqlState, message and statementHandle identify the SQL error; 429 signals rate limiting and 5xx are retried with backoff.
